title: Permutation-symmetric quantum trajectories
authors: Elliot W. Lloyd, Aleksandra A. Ziolkowska, Jonathan Keeling
abstract: We show how one may perform a stochastic unraveling which respects weak permutation symmetry for models of $N$ emitters coupled to a common system (e.g. a cavity mode). For problems involving 2-level emitters, such an unravelling reduces the computational cost from $\mathcal{O}(N^5)$ to $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$, and with additional refinements, allows reduction to $\mathcal{O}(N)$. This significantly increases the range of system sizes for which one can model exact quantum dynamics of such systems. We further show how the method can also be applied to d-level systems, with computational effort scaling as $\mathcal{O}(N^{d(d-1)/2})$, and we show it allows large-N simulations for d=3.
summary: This paper introduces an efficient numerical method for simulating large ensembles of emitters coupled to a common cavity mode. By exploiting weak permutation symmetry, the authors significantly reduce the computational scaling, allowing for exact quantum simulations of much larger system sizes than traditional density matrix methods.
main_problem: Simulating the exact quantum dynamics of large-scale systems of N emitters coupled to a common mode is computationally expensive due to exponential scaling, especially when individual dissipation breaks strong permutation symmetry.
main_result: The authors developed a stochastic unravelling method that respects weak permutation symmetry, reducing computational complexity from O(N^5) to O(N^2) for 2-level systems and even O(N) with refinements, enabling simulations up to N=10^4.
method: A permutation-symmetric quantum trajectory approach using a pseudo-state approach, hybrid unravelling (combining quantum jumps and quantum state diffusion), and a displaced approach to reduce cavity truncation requirements.
model_or_system: The study focuses on N identical emitters (2-level or d-level systems) coupled to a common bosonic cavity mode, specifically applying the method to the Dicke model, the Tavis-Cummings model, and a three-level lasing model.
key_observables: Photon number, photon number fluctuations, cavity field expectation values, and collective spin operators.
important_parameters: Number of emitters (N), coupling strength (g), cavity decay rate (kappa), individual dephasing and loss rates, and the dimension of the emitter levels (d).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be homogeneous (no disorder) to maintain permutation symmetry, and the cavity dissipation is modeled via a single operator.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares photon number and run-time scaling for different unravelling methods; Figure 2 shows photon number dynamics and superradiance in the Tavis-Cummings model; Figure 3 demonstrates inversionless lasing in a three-level system.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of scaling in symmetric systems, presents the mathematical framework for 2-level stochastic unravelling, generalizes the method to d-level systems using group theory (Young diagrams/tableaux), and validates the approach with numerical simulations of the Dicke, Tavis-Cummings, and three-level laser models.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful new tool for simulating large-scale open quantum systems and cavity QED phenomena that were previously computationally inaccessible due to the presence of individual dissipation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Superradiant emission from the Tavis-Cummings model, starting with fully excited 2LS, following [17]. (a,b) Dynamics of photon number for two different values of N. Solid lines show the exact results, while dashed lines show the cumulant prediction from Ref. [17]. (c) Mean square er- ror of the cumulant vs exact results over the time range shown in (a,b), showing a slow decrease with N. (d) Run time for a single trajectory vs system size. See [31] for simulation pa- rameters.

FIG. 1. Dicke model with individual dephasing and dissipa- tion. (a) Photon number as a function of increasing system size. Circles and diamonds show the results of the simple quantum jump approach vs the hybrid displaced approach described in the text. Solid lines compare to cumulant calcu- lations taken from Ref. [32]. Parameters (matching Ref. [32]) are listed in the supplement [31]. Results are averaged over nT = 2000 to 24 trajectories, decreasing with increasing N. (b) Run time of each trajectory simulation. Circles and di- amonds as described above. The solid lines show different power law scalings.

FIG. 3. Inversionless lasing of a three-level system [40] (a) Photon number vs system size, showing convergence toward the mean-field results. (b) Run time per trajectory as a func- tion of system size. The lines show a fit to the expected asymptotic N 4 scaling accounting for dt ∝1/N. See [31] for details of model and simulation parameters.
